content.body:
Bacterial cell structure (prokaryote):
Cell wall (peptidoglycan)
Cell membrane
Cytoplasm
Nucleoid region (circular DNA, no nuclear membrane)
Ribosomes (70S — smaller than eukaryotic)
Flagelum (motility)
Pili (attachment)
Capsula (some species)
Plasmids (extra circular DNA)

NO nucleus, no mitochondria, no chloroplasts, no membrane-bound organelles

content.title: Bacterial Growth
content.body:
Growth curve: Lag phase → Log/Exponential phase → Stationary phase → Death phase
Lag: adaptation, no division
Log: rapid binary fission, doubling every generation time
Stationary: birth rate = death rate (nutrients limited)
Death: death rate > birth rate

Generation time T=20min: after 3h → 2^9=512 cells from 1

N = N₀ × 2^n
N = final number, N₀ = initial number, n = number of generations
n = time / generation time

questions.question: Bacteria are prokaryotes because
questions.answer: they have no membrane-bound nucleus
questions.explanation: Prokaryote = before nucleus. Bacterial DNA floats in nucleoid region without nuclear envelope

questions.question: Bacterial ribosomes are size
questions.answer: 70S (smaller than eukaryotic 80S)
questions.explanation: Bacterial 70S ribosomes are smaller than eukaryotic 80S — target for antibiotics like streptomycin

questions.question: Starting with 1 bacterium, generation time 30min. After 2 hours, number =
questions.explanation: n=2hr/0.5hr=4 generations. N=1×2⁴=16

questions.question: During log phase of bacterial growth
options: cells divide at maximum rate (exponential growth)
questions.answer: cells divide at maximum rate (exponential growth)
questions.explanation: Exponential/log phase: nutrients plentiful, cells divide at maximum rate (doubling each generation)

questions.question: Stationary phase occurs because
options: nutrients exhausted and waste accumulates → birth rate = death rate
questions.answer: nutrients exhausted and waste accumulates → birth rate = death rate
questions.explanation: Resources run out and metabolic waste builds up, balancing growth and death rates

questions.question: Plasmids in bacteria are
options: small circular DNA molecules carrying extra genes (often antibiotic resistance)
questions.answer: small circular DNA molecules carrying extra genes (often antibiotic resistance)
questions.explanation: Plasmids are autonomously replicating circular DNA — often carry antibiotic resistance genes, can be transferred between bacteria

questions.question: Gram-positive bacteria stain purple because
options: their thick peptidoglycan wall retains crystal violet stain
questions.answer: their thick peptidoglycan wall retains crystal violet stain
questions.explanation: Gram staining: thick peptidoglycan retains violet stain (Gram+); thin layer + outer membrane loses stain → Gram-

questions.question: Antibiotics target bacterial 70S ribosomes to
options: block protein synthesis without harming human 80S ribosomes
questions.answer: block protein synthesis without harming human 80S ribosomes
questions.explanation: Selective toxicity: antibiotics like streptomycin target 70S ribosomes (bacteria) but not 80S (human cells)

questions.question: Aseptic technique in microbiology prevents
options: contamination of cultures by unwanted microorganisms
questions.answer: contamination of cultures by unwanted microorganisms
questions.explanation: Aseptic technique: sterile equipment, flame inoculation loop, closed containers — prevents contamination

questions.question: N₀=100, generation time=1hr. After 4 hours, N =
What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Viruses are non-cellular infectious agents. They are NOT cells — they have no ribosomes, no cytoplasm, and cannot carry out metabolism independently. They can only replicate inside a host cell.

Viral structure:
Nucleic acid core (DNA or RNA, but not both)
Protein coat (capsid)
Some have lipid envelope (enveloped viruses)

Viral reproduction cycle:
1. Attachment to specific host cell receptors
2. Entry/injection of nucleic acid
3. Replication using host machinery
4. Assembly of new virus particles
5. Release (lysis or budding)

content.title: Common Viral Diseases
content.body:
HIV: retrovirus (RNA → DNA via reverse transcriptase), attacks CD4+ T cells, causes AIDS
Influenza: RNA virus, mutates rapidly (antigenic variation), annual vaccines needed
Herpes: DNA virus, latent infections
Ebola: RNA virus, haemorrhagic fever, very high mortality
COVID-19: SARS-CoV-2, coronavirus (RNA), spike protein binds ACE2 receptors

content.title: Viruses vs Bacteria
content.body:
Viruses: smaller (20-300nm), no cells, DNA OR RNA, no ribosomes, must infect host
Bacteria: larger (1-10μm), cells, both DNA+RNA, ribosomes, can survive independently
Antibiotics kill bacteria but NOT viruses
Antivirals target viral enzymes (e.g. HIV reverse transcriptase)

questions.question: Viruses are NOT cells because
options: they have no cytoplasm, ribosomes or metabolism
questions.answer: they have no cytoplasm, ribosomes or metabolism
questions.explanation: Viruses are non-living particles outside hosts; they carry only nucleic acid + protein coat, no cellular machinery

questions.question: A virus can contain
options: DNA or RNA but not both
questions.answer: DNA or RNA but not both
questions.explanation: Viruses have either DNA genome or RNA genome — never both

questions.question: HIV targets which cell type?
questions.answer: CD4+ T helper lymphocytes
questions.explanation: HIV uses CD4 receptor to enter T helper cells, destroying the immune system → AIDS

questions.question: Why do new flu vaccines need to be made each year?
questions.answer: Influenza virus mutates rapidly (antigenic drift)
questions.explanation: Influenza RNA mutates frequently, altering surface proteins (haemagglutinin, neuraminidase). Previous antibodies don't recognise new strains

questions.question: Antibiotics are ineffective against viruses because
options: viruses lack the bacterial structures (ribosomes, cell wall) that antibiotics target
questions.answer: viruses lack the bacterial structures (ribosomes, cell wall) that antibiotics target
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial-specific structures. Viruses use host cell machinery, so there are fewer virus-specific targets

questions.question: Reverse transcriptase in HIV converts
questions.answer: RNA to DNA
questions.explanation: HIV is a retrovirus: reverse transcriptase makes DNA copy from RNA genome, which integrates into host DNA

questions.question: Viral attachment to host cell requires
options: complementary surface proteins (virus protein binds specific host receptor)
questions.answer: complementary surface proteins (virus protein binds specific host receptor)
questions.explanation: Viral tropism: viruses can only infect cells with matching receptor molecules on surface

questions.question: The lytic cycle ends with
options: release of new viruses and destruction (lysis) of host cell
questions.answer: release of new viruses and destruction (lysis) of host cell
questions.explanation: Lytic cycle: virus takes over host, replicates, lyses (bursts) host cell releasing hundreds of new virions

questions.question: A prion is
options: a misfolded protein that causes other proteins to misfold (e.g. BSE/CJD)
questions.answer: a misfolded protein that causes other proteins to misfold (e.g. BSE/CJD)
questions.explanation: Prions: infectious proteins with no nucleic acid. Cause spongiform encephalopathies (BSE in cattle, CJD in humans)

questions.question: Herd immunity occurs when
options: enough population is immune that disease cannot spread easily even to unvaccinated
questions.answer: enough population is immune that disease cannot spread easily even to unvaccinated
questions.explanation: Herd immunity threshold (e.g. 95% for measles): most transmission chains broken, protecting vulnerable individuals

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Microbiology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Microbiology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Microbiology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Microbiology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Microbiology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Microbiology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Microbiology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Microbiology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Microbiology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Microbiology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Microbiology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Microbiology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Microbiology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Microbiology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
